# cognito-toolkit (v2) — full reference

> Zero-runtime-dependency, ESM-only micro-library that validates AWS Cognito JWTs for authentication & authorization, plus helpers to obtain OAuth2 `client_credentials` access tokens. Built entirely on Node built-ins (`node:crypto`, global `fetch`, `node:util`). No AWS SDK. Node 20+, latest Bun, latest Deno.

## Concepts

A Cognito user pool signs id and access tokens with RS256 and publishes its public keys as a JWKS document at `https://cognito-idp.<region>.amazonaws.com/<userPoolId>/.well-known/jwks.json`. To authenticate a request you verify the token's signature against that JWKS and check its standard claims. This library does exactly that, offline, with no AWS SDK:

1. Derive the issuer URL from `{region, userPoolId}` (or take an explicit `issuer`).
2. Fetch the JWKS lazily on first use; import each JWK to a `crypto.KeyObject`; cache by `kid`.
3. For each token: split it, decode header + payload, gate `alg` against the `algorithms` policy (default `['RS256']`), check `iss`, resolve the key by `kid` (refreshing the JWKS if the `kid` is unknown — that is the key-rotation signal), verify the signature with `crypto.verify` (digest + padding / `dsaEncoding` come from the matched algorithm, not the header), check `exp` / `nbf`, then run the optional `validate` hook.

Any failure resolves to `null`; malformed input never throws.

The `alg` header is only ever compared against your `algorithms` policy — it never selects how the token is verified — and only asymmetric algorithms are mappable, so `HS*` and `none` can never be verified regardless of policy.

## Install

```sh
npm install cognito-toolkit
```

ESM:

```js
import makeGetUser from 'cognito-toolkit';
```

CommonJS (Node 20.19+ / 22.12+ via `require(esm)`, named export):

```js
const {makeGetUser} = require('cognito-toolkit');
```

## `makeGetUser(pools, globalOptions?)`

Builds a validator for one or more pools.

```js
// single pool
const getUser = makeGetUser({region: 'us-east-1', userPoolId: 'us-east-1_AbCdef'});

// multiple pools — a token from any of them is accepted
const getUser = makeGetUser([
  {region: 'us-east-1', userPoolId: 'us-east-1_AAA'},
  {region: 'us-west-2', userPoolId: 'us-west-2_BBB'}
]);

// explicit issuer (e.g. a custom OIDC provider or a test server)
const getUser = makeGetUser({issuer: 'https://issuer.example.com/pool'});

// with global options
const getUser = makeGetUser(
  {region: 'us-east-1', userPoolId: 'us-east-1_AbCdef'},
  {clockTolerance: 30, minRefreshInterval: 10_000}
);
```

`pools` — one pool object or an array. Each pool: `region` + `userPoolId` (Cognito), or `issuer` (full URL override, trailing slashes trimmed).

`globalOptions`:

- `audience?: string | string[]` — **strongly recommended.** App client id(s). The token's `aud` (id tokens) or `client_id` (access tokens) must match one — rejecting tokens minted for a *different* app client in the same pool. Off by default.
- `tokenUse?: 'access' | 'id' | Array<'access'|'id'>` — **recommended.** Required `token_use`. Rejects a token of the wrong type (e.g. an id token where an access token is expected). Off by default.
- `algorithms?: string[] | ((alg, header) => boolean)` — allowed signing algorithms. Default `['RS256']` (what Cognito uses). The verifiable set is `SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS` — `RS256/384/512`, `PS256/384/512`, `ES256/384/512`. Symmetric (`HS*`) and `none` are never verifiable, so a wider list cannot enable algorithm confusion. Pass a predicate for custom logic.
- `validate?: (payload, header) => boolean | Promise<boolean>` — optional gate run after all built-in checks. Return falsy (or throw) to reject. Use for `scope` or custom claims.
- `throwOnError?: boolean` — when `true`, throws `CognitoAuthError` (with a `.code` like `'token_expired'` / `'wrong_audience'`) instead of resolving `null`, so callers can tell *why* a token failed. Default `false`.
- `fetch?: typeof fetch` — custom fetch; defaults to the global.
- `minRefreshInterval?: number` — minimum ms between per-issuer JWKS refreshes triggered by an unknown `kid`. Guards the upstream endpoint against refresh storms from streams of unknown-`kid` tokens. Default `30000`.
- `clockTolerance?: number` — allowed clock skew in seconds for `exp` / `nbf`. Default `0`.

`SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS` and `CognitoAuthError` are also exported from the main module. The returned validator carries `prime(): Promise<void>` to pre-fetch every issuer's JWKS (e.g. to avoid first-request latency on a serverless cold start).

Returns `(token: string) => Promise<payload | null>` (or, under `throwOnError`, resolves the payload / throws `CognitoAuthError`). The payload is the decoded JWT body (standard Cognito claims such as `sub`, `iss`, `token_use`, `exp`, plus pool-specific claims like `cognito:username`, `cognito:groups`, `email`).

### Security: set `audience` and `tokenUse`

Verifying the signature proves the token is genuinely from the pool — not that it was issued *for your app* or is the *right type*. For any pool with more than one app client, set both `audience` (your app client id(s)) and `tokenUse` (usually `'access'`). Without `audience`, a token minted for any app client in the same user pool is accepted; without `tokenUse`, an id token (which also carries `cognito:groups`) can be used where an access token is expected. Tokens must be the bare JWT — there is no `Bearer ` handling; strip it upstream with `header.replace(/^Bearer\s+/i, '')` if needed.

### Typical usage in a request handler

```js
const authHeader = req.headers.authorization; // "Bearer <jwt>" or "<jwt>"
const token = authHeader?.replace(/^Bearer\s+/i, '');
const user = token && (await getUser(token));
if (!user) {
  res.statusCode = 401;
  return res.end('Unauthorized');
}
// authorize on user['cognito:groups'], user.token_use, etc.
```

### Security notes

- **The `alg` header never selects the algorithm.** It is only gated against your `algorithms` policy; the digest + padding used for verification come from the library's internal map of the matched algorithm. Because that map contains only asymmetric algorithms and the keys are asymmetric public keys from the JWKS, `HS*` and `alg: none` can never be verified — so a permissive `algorithms` policy still cannot enable an algorithm-confusion or `none` attack. The default policy is `['RS256']`.
- **Configurability is safe here because it is server-side.** The `algorithms` policy and `validate` hook are set by your code, not by anything an attacker controls.
- **Issuer is checked before key lookup**, so a token claiming a foreign issuer is rejected without touching the key store.
- Verification uses Node's `crypto.verify`, not a hand-rolled signature routine.

### Non-Cognito OIDC providers

The verifier is provider-agnostic given a JWKS. Point it at any issuer and declare its algorithms:

```js
const getUser = makeGetUser(
  {issuer: 'https://accounts.example.com'},
  {algorithms: ['ES256', 'RS256'], validate: payload => payload.aud === 'my-api'}
);
```

The two `utils/` token holders are Cognito-domain (`/oauth2/token`) oriented but work against any compatible `client_credentials` endpoint.

## `cognito-toolkit/utils/lazy-access-token`

`createLazyAccessToken({url, clientId, secret, fetch?})` → `{authorize, getToken}`.

For service-to-service calls where you need a Cognito `client_credentials` access token from a user-pool **domain** token endpoint (`https://<domain>/oauth2/token`).

```js
import {createLazyAccessToken} from 'cognito-toolkit/utils/lazy-access-token';

const auth = createLazyAccessToken({
  url: 'https://auth.example.com/oauth2/token',
  clientId: process.env.AUTH_CLIENT_ID,
  secret: process.env.AUTH_CLIENT_SECRET
});

const token = await auth.authorize(); // cached until shortly before expiry
const headers = {authorization: token.access_token};
```

- `authorize()` — returns a cached unexpired token, else fetches a fresh one. Refresh fires within 5 minutes of expiry (or at half the lifetime if `expires_in` < 5 min).
- `getToken()` — current token or `null`, no fetch.

State is per-instance; create one holder per credential set.

## `cognito-toolkit/utils/renewable-access-token`

`createRenewableAccessToken({url, clientId, secret, fetch?})` → `{retrieveToken, cancelRenewal, getToken}`.

Like the lazy holder, but it proactively renews on a timer instead of on demand.

```js
import {createRenewableAccessToken} from 'cognito-toolkit/utils/renewable-access-token';

const auth = createRenewableAccessToken({
  url: 'https://auth.example.com/oauth2/token',
  clientId: process.env.AUTH_CLIENT_ID,
  secret: process.env.AUTH_CLIENT_SECRET
});

await auth.retrieveToken();        // fetch once; auto-renews thereafter
const token = auth.getToken();     // always read the live token

// on shutdown
auth.cancelRenewal(true);
```

- `retrieveToken()` — fetches a token and schedules a refresh shortly before expiry. The refresh timer is `unref`ed, so it never keeps the process alive by itself.
- `cancelRenewal(clearToken?)` — cancels the scheduled refresh; pass `true` to also drop the cached token.
- `getToken()` — current token or `null`. The renewal swaps it out over time, so always read it fresh rather than holding a reference.

## Migration from v1

- v1 was CommonJS (`const makeGetUser = require('cognito-toolkit')`) and depended on `jsonwebtoken` + `jwk-to-pem`. v2 is ESM-only, zero-dependency.
- The `makeGetUser(pools)` contract is **unchanged**; only the import changes (`import makeGetUser from 'cognito-toolkit'`, or `const {makeGetUser} = require('cognito-toolkit')` from CJS).
- The token utilities moved from module-level singletons to **factories**. v1 `setCredentials(...) / authorize() / getToken()` becomes `const {authorize, getToken} = createLazyAccessToken({url, clientId, secret})`; v1 `retrieveToken(url, id, secret) / cancelRenewal() / getToken()` becomes `const {retrieveToken, cancelRenewal, getToken} = createRenewableAccessToken({url, clientId, secret})`. This fixes the v1 footgun where only one credential set / pool could exist per process.
- Debug logging moved from the `debug` package to `node:util` `debuglog` — enable with `NODE_DEBUG=cognito-toolkit`.

## Companion middleware

- Express: https://github.com/uhop/cognito-express-middleware
- Koa: https://github.com/uhop/koa-cognito-middleware
